---
title: "Grazing the fjord landscape: interactions between environmental gradients, management and grassland community assemblages"
date: today
bibliography: references.bib
number-sections: true
execute: 
  echo: false
format:
  elsevier-pdf:
    keep-tex: true
    fig-dpi: 800
    # mainfont: "Times New Roman"
    # sansfont: "Times New Roman"
    linestretch: 2
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        \usepackage{lscape}
        \newcommand{\blandscape}{\begin{landscape}}
        \newcommand{\elandscape}{\end{landscape}}
        \usepackage{lineno}
        \linenumbers

      formatting: preprint
      model: 3p
      layout: onecolumn
      cite-style: authoryear

    orcid: https://orcid.org/0000-0003-2223-1857

      - Center for Sustainable Area Management (CeSAM), University of Bergen, Norway

    orcid: https://orcid.org/0000-0002-8609-1091

      - Faculty of Biosciences and Aquaculture, Nord University, Norway
      - Center for Sustainable Area Management (CeSAM), University of Bergen, Norway

    orcid: https://orcid.org/0000-0002-3749-6906
    affiliations: Department of Geoecology, Institute of Botany of the Czech Academy of Sciences, Czech Republic
  - name: Inger Elisabeth Måren
    orcid: https://orcid.org/0000-0002-3964-9144

---

```{r ScriptSource}
#| include: false
source("scripts/NBR_TradMod_FjordSysCANCOR.R", local = knitr::knit_global())
```


```{r setup, include=FALSE}
op = function(x, d=1) sprintf(paste0("%1.",d,"f"), x)
theme_set(theme_bw(base_size = 5))
```

# ABSTRACT {.unnumbered}

Pasture-based husbandry has shaped plant and animal communities of high diversity that support multiple ecosystem functions.
However, the drivers of community composition are yet to be fully understood due to complex interactions between management and underlying environmental gradients.
Here we present an attempt at disentangling these relationships and their influences on grassland community assemblages across multiple spatial scales in the agroecological system of the Nordhordland UNESCO Biosphere Reserve, Western Norway.
We used both canonical correlation analyses and redundancy analyses to investigate
1) links between the regional environment, the landscape matrix, field management and the fine-scale environment of 29 grazed fields, and
2) links between these factors and dominant grass, forb and dung-associated beetle assemblages.
The composition of the landscape matrix was influenced by the regional environment, as land use intensity increased with closer proximity to fjords.
Field management was weakly correlated with opposing gradients of vegetation height and bulk density, suggesting that farmers adapt livestock type, stocking density and the size of grazing area according to grasslands' productivity.
Only the fine-scale environment explained significant proportions of community assemblage variances, which responded to gradients of vegetation height, soil penetration and bulk density respectively.
Dominant grass and dung-associated beetle assemblages were also correlated with the regional environment and field management, indicating the existence of more complex interactions which were only superficially captured in our analyses.
Overall, our results suggest that the effects of diversified grazing management on grassland community assemblages of the Nordhordland UNESCO Biosphere Reserve may be overridden by stronger environmental gradients at local scale.

Keywords: agroecology; livestock; plant community; dung-associated beetle community; Nordhordland UNESCO Biosphere Reserve

\setcounter{section}{0}

# INTRODUCTION

Plant and animal communities of semi-natural grasslands are the result of a long history of management involving mutual interactions between human, domestic grazers and the local environment [@denglerBiodiversityPalaearcticGrasslands2014; @ellisLandUseEcological2021].
These communities have ensured the long-term production of livestock, either by directly supplying the forage resource (e.g. grasses, forbs) [@collombCorrelationFattyAcids2002; @frenchSpeciesCompositionDetermines2017], or by supporting forage provision through for instance nutrient cycling (e.g. dung-associated beetles, soil fauna) [@bardgettBelowgroundBiodiversityEcosystem2014; @nicholsEcologicalFunctionsEcosystem2008].
In Europe, recent research has shown how agricultural intensification has led to the simplification of semi-natural grassland communities, and the subsequent negative impacts on biodiversity and ecosystem functioning [@allanLandUseIntensification2015; @schilsPermanentGrasslandsEurope2022].
However, in low-intensity grazing systems, effects of management on plant and animal communities are not as consistent [@scohierHowSheepAffect2012; @wangGlobalMetaanalysesResponse2019], and the influence of underlying environmental gradients is stronger [@denglerBiodiversityPalaearcticGrasslands2014].
Disentangling these interactions is therefore necessary to understand to what extent farmers can affect the productivity and diversity of their fields [@redlichDisentanglingEffectsClimate2022].

The influences of environmental gradients and management on plant and animal communities in semi-natural grasslands operate across multiple spatial scales [@denglerBiodiversityPalaearcticGrasslands2014; @petersCrossScaleInteractions2007].
At the regional scale, variation in climate and elevation generates gradients of abiotic stress and resource availability, affecting species distribution through habitat filtering and mitigating biotic interactions within communities [@conradiResourceAvailabilityDetermines2017; @fridleyLongerGrowingSeasons2016; @klanderudImportanceBioticVs2015].
The resulting patterns of primary productivity determine ecosystems' resilience to disturbance, and by extension their capacity for sustaining different types of land use [@piipponenGlobalTrendsGrassland2022].
At the landscape scale, connectivity between semi-natural habitats contributes to the diversification of species pools [@billeterIndicatorsBiodiversityAgricultural2007; @zulkaSpeciesRichnessDry2014] and ensures the stability of functional networks [@fantinatoCooccurrenceDifferentGrassland2019; @neffCrossscaleEffectsLand2019].
Agricultural intensification disrupts species migration and the continuity of the landscape matrix, leading to the simplification of natural communities and their ecological interactions [@kareivaConnectingLandscapePatterns1995; @leprovostContrastingResponsesBelowground2021; @seiboldArthropodDeclineGrasslands2019].
At the field scale, the strongest selective pressure usually comes from land use [@neffCrossscaleEffectsLand2019] and varies in direction and magnitude according to the type and intensity of the management [@gaujourFactorsProcessesAffecting2012].
In grazing systems, multiple factors such as stocking density, grazer selectivity or fertilisation can affect species dominance by favouring competitive or ruderal strategies [@koernerChangeDominanceDetermines2018; @olffEffectsHerbivoresGrassland1998].
Manure deposition and animal excreta also support the activity of a diversified coprophilous beetle community [@daoudiCompositionPhenologyBeetle2022; @sigsgaardEnvironmentalDNAMetabarcoding2021], which facilitates the nutrient cycling process between the livestock, the soil and the vegetation [@nicholsEcologicalFunctionsEcosystem2008].
Lastly, the fine-scale environment in the fields, including gradients of topographic (e.g. slope angle, aspect), edaphic (e.g. soil moisture, bulk density, nutrient availability) and biotic (e.g. vegetation height, litter cover) parameters, determines the resources availability for the plant community [@fayGrasslandProductivityLimited2015; @klimekPlantSpeciesRichness2007] and arthropod ecological niches [@broseBottomupControlCarabid2003; @coleInfluenceFinescaleHabitat2010].
Some factors, such as topography, are inherent to the local context, while other factors such as soil physicochemical properties or vegetation structure are also influenced by management. 
For instance, livestock trampling and grazing affecting soil compaction and vegetation height respectively [@mayelHowDoesGrassland2021; @zhangSoilPhysicalChemical2019].

A suitable framework for investigating cross-scale interactions within agroecological systems is the UNESCO World Network of Biosphere Reserves (WNBR) and its associated Man and Biosphere (MAB) program [@unescoManBiosphereProgramme2025; @unescoNewRoadmapMan2017], which offer a platform of international collaboration and knowledge-sharing for the regional implementation of the Agenda 2030 Sustainable Development Goals [@barracloughGlobalKnowledgeAction2023; @unitednationsTransformingOurWorld2015].
In Western Norway, the Nordhordland UNESCO Biosphere Reserve epitomises the agroecological landscape of the fjord region [@kalandNordhordlandBiosphereReserve2018], which has been shaped by a long history of small-scale farming and livestock grazing [@austadHumaninfluencedVegetationTypes1991; @hjelleLongtermChangesRegional2018].
The Nordhordland small-scale grazing systems include different types of farm structure, ranging from extensive sheep and goat farming to more production-oriented mixed dairy-beef systems [@potthoffNorwegianLowintensityOvine2023].
Farmers usually rely on rotational grazing to limit the stocking density on individual fields and allow grasslands to recover [@indrelidFarmingSystemIts1988].
These different management practices would be expected to generate taxonomic and/or functional divergences in plant and animal communities of semi-natural grasslands [@boavistaEffectsGrazingRegimes2019; @lenoirEffectsTimingGrazing2010; @tothLivestockTypeMore2016].
However, the agroecological system of the Nordhordland UNESCO Biosphere Reserve is also characterised by strong environmental gradients associated with the fjord context, including oceanity, topography and climate [@nesjeFjordsNorwayComplex2009].
In these circumstances, the structure and composition of communities are likely to be affected by multiple interactions between grazing management and different levels of environmental variation, which, to our knowledge, have yet to be investigated in the agroecological landscapes of the fjord region.

In this study, we relied on a spatially-explicit approach to explore (1) links between field management and environmental gradients and (2) their respective influences on dominant assemblages of semi-natural grasslands in the Nordhordland UNESCO Biosphere Reserve.
We classified the explanatory factors into four main spatial scales, based on a conceptual representation of the small-scale grazing systems in the fjord region of Western Norway (@fig-fjordconcept):
the **regional scale**, representing the climate and landform gradient associated with the fjord context; 
the **landscape scale**, related to the composition of the landscape matrix surrounding the grazing fields; 
the **field scale**, characterising the grazing management of the field; 
and finally the **fine scale**, including variables related to vegetation and soil structure, topography and nutrients.
We selected grass three indicator communities in relation to their key contributions to forage provision in semi-natural grasslands:
grass and forb species, as direct determinant of forage availability and nutritional quality;
and dung-associated beetle families, as indirect support of forage production through their role in the nutrient cycling process.

![Conceptual representation of expected cross-scale interactions between the regional environment, the landscape matrix, field management, the fine-scale environment and dominant grass, forb and dung-associated beetle assemblages of the semi-natural grasslands of the Nordhordland UNESCO Biosphere Reserve, Norway. Explanatory variables included in the analysis after selection are in black typeface and those excluded are in grey. For description and selection of explanatory variables and response assemblages, see the Material and Methods section. Credits: figure created on Inkscape v. 1.1 (Inkscape Developers, https://inkscape.org).](figure1.png){#fig-fjordconcept width=100%}

# MATERIAL AND METHODS

## Study area

The Nordhordland UNESCO Biosphere Reserve is located in the fjord region of Western Norway, between the Bergen region to the south and the Sognefjord, Europe's deepest and longest fjord, to the north [@kalandNordhordlandBiosphereReserve2018]. 
The area is characterised by strong elevation and climate gradients from west to east, with average July temperature dropping from 15.9 to 8.3°C and annual precipitation increasing from 1550 to 4800 mm [@ketzlerClimateNorway2021; @meteorologiskinstituttNorskKlimaservicesenter2023]. 

In the summers of 2019 and 2020, we conducted an ecological survey on 29 grazing fields distributed across the southern part of the Nordhordland UNESCO Biosphere Reserve, all located below 250 m above sea level [@fig-sitemap; @demeauxJustGrazeIt2024].
Our sites were "infields" (*innmarksbeite*), defined by the Norwegian AR5 land cover classification system as "*agricultural land that cannot be harvested mechanically* \[with\] *at least 50% of the area* \[...\] *covered with cultured grass or pasture-tolerant herbs*" [@ahlstromAR5Klassifikasjonssystem2019]. 
All fields had been grazed uninterruptedly for at least 10 years, and had been grazed by the same livestock type, either sheep, goats or cattle, for at least the last 5 years. 
According to the farmers, most fields had been continuously grazed for at least 50 years; 
three had short gaps in their grazing history (5 years or under), and one had a long gap (20 years). 
Grazing intensity was low, with a maximum stocking rate of 0.6 yearly unit per ha. 
Twenty-one sites were enriched at least occasionally with individual or simultaneous use of artificial fertiliser, manure or shellsand-lime mix. 
To the knowledge of the farmers, no field had been resown and/or plowed since at least the 1970s, and only four out of the 29 sites received upgraded drainage between 1990 and 2010.

![Location of the study sites in the Nordhordland UNESCO Biosphere Reserve (blue line), Western Norway. Colour indicates the type of livestock on the grazing fields: black for cattle, grey for sheep, white for goats. Credits: map created in QGIS v. 3.12, CSR ETRS89 (QGIS Association, http://www.qgis.org); background orthophotography (both main and miniature) from the Norwegian Mapping Authority (https://www.kartverket.no) CC BY 4.0.](figure2.png){#fig-sitemap width=50%}

\newpage

## Data collection

### Regional environment

``` {r CorrelationTempRangeLongitude}
#| message: false
#| warning: false

oceanity <- left_join(climate_infield, siteinfo_full)
lm_oceanity <- lm(maxtempJuly-mintempJan ~ EPSG.25832_X, data = oceanity)
summary_oceanity <- summary(lm_oceanity)

```

The regional environment explanatory set encompasses climatic and geographical variables operating at broad spatial scales.
Average annual precipitation, maximum and average July temperatures, and minimum and average January temperatures, were obtained from 1 km^2^ daily projections provided by the Norwegian Centre for Climate Services over the period 2010-2020 [@wongGridded112016]. 
Average annual temperature range, calculated as the difference between maximum July temperature and minimum January temperature, showed a strong linear relationship with longitude (adjusted R^2^ = `r op(summary_oceanity$adj.r.squared, 2)`, F = `r op(summary_oceanity$fstatistic[1], 0)`, p-value = `r op(summary_oceanity$coefficients[2,4], 3)`) and was used as a proxy for oceanity.
For sites not covered by the daily projections, data were extrapolated from averaging values of neighbouring cells with the R software package *terra* (v. 1.7.29) [@hijmansTerraSpatialData2023; @rcoreteamLanguageEnvironmentStatistical2024].

### Landscape matrix

The landscape explanatory set represents the composition of the landscape matrix around semi-natural grasslands.
We calculated land use percent cover in a 1 km radius area around each field from land cover data provided by the Norwegian Institute of Bioeconomy Research (NIBIO) [@norskinstituttforbiookonominibioKildenArealinformasjon2023].
Land cover was categorised according to the AR5 land cover classification system [@ahlstromAR5Klassifikasjonssystem2019], that we simplified into eight categories:
forest area, including managed and non-managed; 
cultivated area, either fully or partially; 
infield area, i.e. enclosed, productive grazing area (permanent grasslands); 
outfield area, i.e. unenclosed, low-productivity grazing area (shrublands or heathlands); 
wetland area; 
artificial area.

### Field management

The field management explanatory set encompasses factors affecting the grazing pressure on the field.
We conducted phone interviews with 23 farmers from October 2021 to January 2022 to collect information on field management. 
For the 3 out of 26 farmers who were unable or unwilling to reply to our survey, we used data from a similar interview series conducted in 2018 [@reiersenBaerekraftVestnorskLandbruk2019]. 
We asked the farmers for the following elements: 
the type of livestock which has been grazing the site for the past 5 years; 
the size of the flock, including adults and young; 
the grazing period on the visited field; 
the total surface area of infields grazed by the animals on the farm; 
the size of the grazing field we selected as a survey site.

Stocking density is usually calculated as follows: 

$$
\text{Stocking density} = \frac{\text{number animals}\times{\text{standard livestock unit}}}{\text{area}\times{\text{grazing period}}}
$$ 

However, estimating stocking density for individual fields was challenging, as  most respondents could not give us accurate field occupation periods due to the regular transfers of livestock during the season.
We thus arranged the formula to calculate instead an average stocking density for the farms' total infield area over a year:

$$
\text{Average stocking density} = \frac{\text{Nadults}\times{\text{standard  LU}} + \text{Nyoung}\times{\text{young LU}} }{\text{total infield area of the farm}}
$$ 

We defined the reference livestock unit (LU) for young sheep and goats as half the value of adult (0.05 LU). 
The total infield area within each farm was retrieved from the map of agricultural properties made by the Norwegian Institute of Bioeconomy Research (https://gardskart.nibio.no/). 
We included both areas classified as infields (*innmark*) and unclassified areas which were known as grazing fields from the farmer and/or from field observation. 
For two farms (IG1 and IG2) where livestock was moved to mountain summer farms every year, we calculated average stocking density for the time period when animals were in the lowlands.

### Fine-scale environment

The fine-scale environment encompasses biological and environmental factors characterising vegetation structure, soil texture and soil nutrient availability.
The study design is based on @demeauxJustGrazeIt2024.
We visited each of the 29 sites once, starting in mid-June at the westernmost sites and ending in late July at the easternmost sites to account for the spring bloom time difference [@karlsenVariabilityStartGrowing2007]. 
On each site, we laid out a nested sampling design within a representative area of 400 m^2^, preferentially 20 m x 20 m but when necessary 40 m x 10 m, avoiding rocks, waterlogged ground, trees and field edges.
According to the same exclusion rules, we set three plots of 3 m x 3 m in the sampling area, at least 6 m apart from each other (@fig-sampling). 
Each plot was divided into nine subplots of 1 m^2^, five non-destructive and four destructive respectively.
In each non-destructive subplot (15 per site), we evaluated the average percent cover of bare ground, rock, litter and bryophytes, and we estimated average vegetation height from three random measurements within the subplot.

![Schematic representation of the nested sampling design applied in a representative area of each site. Field sampling was carried out on the same day except for beetle trapping. Credits: figure created using Inkscape v. 1.1 (Inkscape Developers, https://inkscape.org).](figure3.png){#fig-sampling width=50%}

Destructive subplots were dedicated to the determination of soil physicochemical properties.
We characterised soil structure using three complementary measurements: 
soil resistance to penetration, induced by root mat density and soil texture; 
bulk density, as a proxy for soil compaction;
and gravimetric water content, indicating water holding capacity. 
We measured soil resistance to penetration twice in each destructive subplot (24 per site), by dropping a sharpened metal rod of 43.4 cm length (diam. 2.2 cm; weight 1.3 kg) from 1 m above ground through a PVC tube to ensure vertical fall. 
The visible standing part of the stick was measured and subtracted from the total stick length to obtain the length of penetration into the soil. 
Before dropping the stick, the surrounding vegetation around a 5 cm radius was cleared to prevent resistance from the aboveground vegetation. 
The tip was regularly sharpened and remeasured to account for the decrease in length, which was of 1.6 % (0.7 cm) by the end of the sampling season.

For bulk density and gravimetric water content, we sampled three soil cores with PVC tubes of 57.23 cm^3^ (diam. 3.7 cm, height 10 cm) in each destructive subplot (36 per site). 
To limit soil compression during collection, we sharpened the edges of the PVC tubes and cut the surrounding root mat with a knife while pushing down the cores. 
Samples were covered with plastic foil and stored at 6°C before processing in the lab. 
If necessary, the soil volume of each core was corrected for holes or unfilled space prior to weighing.
We discarded cores with less than 50 cm^3^ of soil to prevent inaccurate measurements of bulk density and soil moisture, which represented approx. 20% of the total collected cores.

Gravimetric water content is calculated as the difference in proportion between the soil moist weight and the soil dry weight, and bulk density as the soil dry weight related to its volume:


$$
\text{Gravimetric water content}\; (\%) = \frac{\text{Core moist weight}\;(g) - \text{Core dry weight}\;(g)}{\text{Core moist weight}\;(g)}*100
$$

$$
\text{Bulk density}\;(g/cm^3) = \frac{\text{Core dry weight}\;(g)}{\text{Core volume}\;(cm^3)}
$$

Moist weight is usually measured from freshly sampled soil.
However, as weather conditions varied considerably during our sampling period, we standardised the measurement by weighing soil 48h after water saturation in the laboratory. 
Dry weight was obtained after 24h of drying in the oven at 105°C. 
After data filtering of low volumes and some negative values linked to a weighing balance dysfunction during lab processing, all sites had at least 9 measurements of bulk density and gravimetric water content each.

For chemical analyses, we sampled soil with a stainless steel cylindrical core of 200 cm^3^ (diam. 10.5 cm; height 12 cm) in each destructive subplot. 
The soil was stored at 4°C before being sieved and merged at the plot level (3 per site).
We sent 0.5 L of each pooled sample to a certified analysis laboratory to determine total nitrogen content with modified Kjeldahl method (NS-EN 13654-1:2890) [@bremnerDeterminationNitrogenSoil1960], and available phosphorus concentration with ammonium lactacte extraction method [@brayDeterminationTotalOrganic1945].

### Dominant grass, forb and dung-associated beetle assemblages

In each non-destructive subplot (15 per site), all plant species were identified using the Nordic flora [@mossbergGyldendalsStoreNordiske2018] and attributed an average percent cover by one of the same two observers.
When necessary, specimens were collected, coded and brought for microscopic or expert identification.
In each destructive subplot (12 per site), we collected dung-associated beetles with dung-baited pitfall traps set for one full week between early July and early August.
Clumped trapping compensated for the short sampling period and should not lead to spatial autocorrelation of catches [@bakerEvaluatingSpatialAutocorrelation2006; @wardEffectsPitfallTrap2001].
Traps were made of white plastic cups of 1 L (diam. 10.5 cm, height 11.5 cm) filled to 1/4 with salted water, with a drop of neutral detergent to break water surface tension. 
The cups were covered with a square metal grid of 2 cm x 2 cm mesh size pegged into the ground, on which we put a ball of about fist size of previously-frozen cattle dung.
The dung ball was placed at the centre of the grid, at least 3 cm from the edge of the cup to provide enough space for beetles to fall in.
Beetles were stored in 70% ethanol and identified to the family level using the identification key from @duffBeetlesBritainIreland2012a.
The sampling design resulted in a spatial clumping of pitfall traps, but it was not deemed to cause spatial autocorrelation or affect our abundance data [@bakerEvaluatingSpatialAutocorrelation2006; @wardEffectsPitfallTrap2001].

## Data analysis

First, we used canonical correlation analysis (CCA) to investigate
1) links between sets of explanatory variables characterising the regional environment, the landscape matrix, field management and the fine-scale environment of semi-natural grasslands, and
2) links between these explanatory sets and dominant grass, forb and dung-associated beetle assemblages (following scheme in @fig-fjordconcept).
Canonical correlation analysis is a multivariate statistical method which allows to summarise relationships from high-dimensional datasets [@hotellingRelationsTwoSets1992].
Specifically, it searches for linear projections showing the maximum co-variance between two sets of quantitative variables and is thus suitable for exploring relationships between environmental gradients, field management and community assemblages symmetrically.
Second, we used redundancy analysis (RDA) to investigate the directional processes which underlie relationships between explanatory sets, from the broader to the finer scale, and between explanatory sets and community assemblages.
Redundancy analysis is a constrained ordination method, which performs multiple linear regression to multivariate data based on Euclidean distance [@legendreNumericalEcology2012].
Contrary to canonical correlation analysis, which projects total variation among two symmetrical sets, redundancy analysis only explores the variation explained by an explanatory matrix over a response matrix.
It is therefore suitable for investigating the top-down effects of environmental gradients and field management on community assemblages and to disentangle the influence of individual variables.

Our analyses were performed on data averaged at the site-level, because we aim to understand processes which affect representative assemblages of grazing fields rather than within-site variation.
Site-level averaging may cause a loss of sensitivity regarding interactions between the fine-scale environment and dominant grass and forb assemblages, which operates at finer spatial ranges.
Therefore, we led supplementary analyses at plot-level in these two cases while controlling for the structure of the nested sampling design.
This supplementary analysis did not apply for dominant dung-associated beetle assemblages, whose relationship with the fine-scale environment operates at higher spatial range due to their greater mobility.

Prior to analyses, explanatory variables (environmental gradients and field management) were filtered to meet the assumptions of the canonical correlation analysis and the redundancy analysis [@tabachnickUsingMultivariateStatistics2014] and scaled to 0 mean and standard deviation 1 to account for unit differences.
Livestock type, the only qualitative variable of our study, was transformed into two dummy numeric variables, Sheep and Cattle respectively.
We removed variables with strongly skewed distributions or with obvious outliers and tested variable collinearity within each set with the Spearman's rank correlation test.
For a correlation coefficient of 0.7 or higher, we selected variables with the closest distribution to normal.
In the regional environment set, we removed average January temperature which was strongly correlated with annual temperature range (Spearman's rank correlation coefficient of 0.93).
In the field management set, the number of adult animals grazing on field was removed due to a clear outlier.
In the fine-scale environment set, we removed litter percent cover, which showed a highly skewed distribution, and we removed gravimetric water content, loss of ignition, total nitrogen content and humus content which were negatively correlated with bulk density (Spearman's rank correlation coefficients ranging from 0.78 to 1).
See @tbl-varsum for the statistical summary of variables kept for the analyses.

Community assemblage data were percent cover for grass (Poaceae) and forb species, and number of catches for beetle families.
"Dominant" plant species and beetle families were characterised by criteria of frequency, i.e. presence in at least one-third of the sites, and average abundance, i.e. minimum plant species percent cover of 1% and minimum beetle family occurrence of 3.
In total, eight species of grasses, seven species of forbs and five families of beetles met these two criteria and were selected for the analyses (@fig-fjordconcept).
We performed Hellinger transformation on community data to lower the weight of variables with low counts and many zeros [@borcardNumericalEcology2011; @legendreEcologicallyMeaningfulTransformations2001].

For the canonical correlation analyses, the statistical significance of canonical correlation coefficients rho was tested with the asymptotic approximation of Hotelling-Lawley Trace [@mullerPracticalMethodsComputing1984].
For the redundancy analyses, the statistical significance of models, axes and marginal terms was tested with ANOVA-like permutation tests (n = 999) [@legendreTestingSignificanceCanonical2011; @legendreNumericalEcology2012].
For the supplementary redundancy analyses between the fine-scale environment and dominant grass and forb assemblages, we restricted permutations at plot-level to prevent pseudo-replication [@andersonPermutationTestsMultifactorial2003].

All statistical analyses were performed in R (v.4.4.1) with R-Studio (v.2025.05.1) [@positteamRStudioIntegratedDevelopment2025; @rcoreteamLanguageEnvironmentStatistical2024]. 
The canonical correlation analyses and the related statistical tests were made with the *CCA* [@gonzalezCCACanonicalCorrelation2023] and *CCP* [@menzelCCPSignificanceTests2022] packages. 
The redundancy analyses were performed with the *vegan* package [@oksanenVeganCommunityEcology2024] and their visual representations were made with the *ggord* package [@beckGgordOrdinationPlots2022].
The restricted permutations for the supplementary analyses were performed with the *permute* package [@simpsonPermuteFunctionsGenerating2011].

\scriptsize

```{r tbl-varsum}
#| tbl-cap: "Summary statistics of variables selected for the regional-scale, landscape-scale, field-scale and fine-scale explanatory sets after data filtering. Not applicable for Sheep and Cattle variables which are dummy numeric."
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Packages
library(gt)
library(tidyverse)
# library(kableExtra)

# Join all variables
allvar <- purrr::reduce(list(subset(regional, select = -c(JanTemp)), landscape, field, subset(fine, select = -c(GWC, LOI, Humus, Nitrog))), dplyr::full_join)

# Summary statistics
varsum <- allvar |> 
  summarise(across(where(is.numeric), .fns =
                     list(Mean = mean,
                          sd = sd,
                          Min = min,
                          Max = max
                     ))) |>
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_sep = "_", names_to = c( "Variable", ".value")) |>
  mutate(Set = ifelse(Variable == "AnnPreci" |
                        Variable == "Elev" |
                        Variable == "FjordDist" |
                        Variable == "TempRange" |
                        Variable == "JulTemp", "Regional", ifelse(
                          Variable == "Cultivated" |
                          Variable == "Forest" | 
                          Variable == "Infield" | 
                          Variable == "Artificial" |
                          Variable == "Outfield" |
                          Variable == "Wetland", "Landscape", ifelse(
                            Variable == "FieldArea" |
                            Variable == "StockDens" |
                            Variable == "Cattle" |
                            Variable == "Sheep", "Field", "Fine"
    )
  ))) |> 
  mutate(
    Variable = dplyr::recode(
      Variable,
          "Elev" = "Elevation (masl)",
    "AnnPreci" = "Annual precipitation (mm)",
    "JulTemp" = "Maximum July temperature (°C)",
    "TempRange" = "Annual temperature range (°C)",
    "FjordDist" = "Distance to fjord (m)",
    "Cultivated" = "Cultivated area (%)",
    "Forest" = "Forest area (%)",
    "Infield" = "Infield area (%)",
    "Outfield" = "Outfield area (%)",
    "Artificial" = "Infrastructure area (%)",
    "Wetland" = "Wetland area (%)",
    "FieldArea" = "Field site area (ha)",
    "StockDens" = "Stocking density (LU/ha)",
    "Bryo" = "Bryophyte cover (%)",
    "MeanHeight" = "Sward height (cm)",
    "BD" = "Bulk density (g/cm3)",
    "pH" = "pH",
    "Phosph" = "Available phosphorus (mg/100g)",
    "SoilPene" = "Soil penetration (cm)"
    )
  ) |> 
  arrange(Variable)

varsum |>
 # gt(groupname_col = "explanatory", rowname_col = "response") |>
 # tab_options(row_group.as_column = TRUE) |>
 gt(groupname_col = "Set", rowname_col = "Variable") |>
  # cols_label(
  #   Variance = "variance",
  #   PrF = "p-value",
  #   Df = "df",
  #   adj.r.squared = "adjRsq"
  # ) |>
  fmt_number(
    decimals = 1,
  ) |>
  fmt_number(
    decimals = 0,
    sep_mark = " ",
    rows = c("Elevation (masl)", "Annual precipitation (mm)", "Distance to fjord (m)")
  ) |>
  fmt_number(
    decimals = 2,
    rows = c("Stocking density (LU/ha)")
  ) |>
  sub_values(
    rows = c("Sheep", "Cattle"),
    fn = function(x) x >=0 & x <= 1,
    replacement = "-"
  ) |> 
  sub_missing(missing_text = "-") |>
  row_group_order(
    groups = c("Regional", "Landscape", "Field", "Fine")
  ) |> 
  opt_stylize(style = 3, color = 'gray') |>
  tab_options(
    table.font.size = 11
  )
```

\normalsize

# RESULTS

We recorded `r op(nrow(filter(vege_average, FunctionalGroup == "grasses")), 0)` grass species, among which eight were present in at least 10 sites and with a minimum average cover of 1% per subplot: 
*Agrostis capillaris* (`r op(filter(vege_average, Species == "Agrostis capillaris")$PlantSp_cover)`% of average quadrat cover), 
*Festuca rubra* (`r op(filter(vege_average, Species == "Festuca rubra")$PlantSp_cover)`%), 
*Holcus lanatus* (`r op(filter(vege_average, Species == "Holcus lanatus")$PlantSp_cover)`%), 
*Poa pratensis* (`r op(filter(vege_average, Species == "Poa pratensis")$PlantSp_cover)`%), 
*Deschampsia cespitosa* (`r op(filter(vege_average, Species == "Deschampsia cespitosa")$PlantSp_cover)`%), 
*Anthoxanthum odoratum* (`r op(filter(vege_average, Species == "Anthoxanthum odoratum")$PlantSp_cover)`%), 
*Lolium perenne* (`r op(filter(vege_average, Species == "Lolium perenne")$PlantSp_cover)`%) and 
*Deschampsia flexuosa* (`r op(filter(vege_average, Species == "Deschampsia flexuosa")$PlantSp_cover)`%). 
We recorded `r op(nrow(filter(vege_average, FunctionalGroup == "forbs")), 0)` forb species, among which seven species were present in at least 10 sites and with a minimum average cover of 1% per subplot: 
*Trifolium repens* (`r op(filter(vege_average, Species == "Trifolium repens")$PlantSp_cover)`%), 
*Rumex acetosa* (`r op(filter(vege_average, Species == "Rumex acetosa")$PlantSp_cover)`%), 
*Galium saxatile* (`r op(filter(vege_average, Species == "Galium saxatile")$PlantSp_cover)`%), 
*Potentilla erecta* (`r op(filter(vege_average, Species == "Potentilla erecta")$PlantSp_cover)`%), 
*Ranunculus repens* (`r op(filter(vege_average, Species == "Ranunculus repens")$PlantSp_cover)`%), 
*Achillea millefolium* (`r op(filter(vege_average, Species == "Achillea millefolium")$PlantSp_cover)`%) and 
*Ranunculus acris* (`r op(filter(vege_average, Species == "Ranunculus acris")$PlantSp_cover)`%). 
Finally, we collected a total of 45 685 beetles belonging to 14 families, among which five were found in at least 10 sites and with a minimum of 3 catches on average per pitfall trap: 
the Staphylinidae family (25 161 individuals, `r op(filter(beetle_average, BeetleFamilies == "Staphylinidae")$BeetleFam_abundance)`% of the catches), 
the Ptiliidae family (11 336 individuals, `r op(filter(beetle_average, BeetleFamilies == "Ptiliidae")$BeetleFam_abundance)`% of the catches), 
the Hydrophilidae family (6 322 individuals, `r op(filter(beetle_average, BeetleFamilies == "Hydrophilidae")$BeetleFam_abundance)`% of the catches),
the Scarabaeidae family (1 128 individuals, `r op(filter(beetle_average, BeetleFamilies == "Scarabaeidae")$BeetleFam_abundance)`% of the catches) and
the Carabidae family (1 058 individuals, `r op(filter(beetle_average, BeetleFamilies == "Carabidae")$BeetleFam_abundance)`% of the catches).

## Relationships between environmental gradients and management

The regional environment and the landscape matrix were significantly correlated (rho = `r op(envar$rho[1], 2)`, F approximation of Hotelling-Lawley trace F~30,82~ = `r op(envar$approx[1], 2)`, p-value = `r op(envar$p.value[1], 3)`) (@fig-cancorenvi; @tbl-cancorenv) and the regional environment explained a significant proportion of the landscape matrix variance (adjusted R^2^ = `r op(rdastat_envi$adj.r.squared[1], 2)`, F~5~ = `r op(rdastat_envi$F[1], 2)`, p-value = `r op(rdastat_envi$PrF[1], 3)` (@tbl-rdaregio).
The first axis of the RDA model was significant (F = `r op(rdastat_envi$F[3], 2)`, p-value = `r op(rdastat_envi$PrF[3], 3)`) and mainly driven by the distance to the closest fjord (F = `r op(rdastat_envi$F[9], 2)`, p-value = `r op(rdastat_envi$PrF[9], 3)`).
Fjord proximity was associated with a decrease of forest and outfield areas and an increase of cultivated and infield areas (@fig-figure5a).

The canonical correlation coefficient between field management and the fine-scale environment was not significant (F~24,70~ = `r op(envar$approx[6], 2)`, p-value = `r op(envar$p.value[6], 3)`) (@fig-cancor[A]; @tbl-cancorenv), but field management still explained a marginal proportion of the fine-scale environment variance (R~adj~^2^ = `r op(rdastat_envi$adj.r.squared[48], 2)`, F~4~ = `r op(rdastat_envi$F[48], 2)`, p-value = `r op(rdastat_envi$PrF[48], 3)`) (@tbl-rdafield).
The first axis of the RDA model was significant (F = `r op(rdastat_envi$F[50], 2)`, p-value = `r op(rdastat_envi$PrF[50], 3)`) and was driven by livestock type (F = `r op(rdastat_envi$F[52], 2)`, p-value = `r op(rdastat_envi$PrF[52], 3)`), field area (F = `r op(rdastat_envi$F[54], 2)`, p-value = `r op(rdastat_envi$PrF[54], 3)`) and stocking density (F = `r op(rdastat_envi$F[55], 2)`, p-value = `r op(rdastat_envi$PrF[55], 3)`).
Sheep grazing, bigger grazing area and lower stocking density were associated with greater soil compaction and shorter vegetation (@fig-figure5b).

:::{#fig-cancor layout="[[1], [1,1,1]]"}
![](figure4a.png){#fig-cancorenvi width=100%}

![](figure4b.png){#fig-cancorgrass width=100%}

![](figure4c.png){#fig-cancorforb width=100%}

![](figure4d.png){#fig-cancorbeetle width=100%}

Summary of canonical correlation analyses (a) between explanatory sets (regional environment, landscape matrix, field management, fine-scale environment), and between explanatory sets and (b) dominant grass assemblages, (c) dominant forb assemblages and (d) dominant dung-associated beetle assemblages in the semi-natural grasslands of the Nordhordland UNESCO Biosphere Reserve. Correlation coefficient rho is provided for the first dimension per default and for all other significant dimensions. Dotted lines represent non-significant relationships, and arrow thickness is proportional to the rho value. Significance levels are: ° for p-value \< 0.1; \* for p-value \< 0.05; \*\* for p-value \< 0.01; \*\*\* for p-value \< 0.001.
:::

:::{#fig-rdaenvi layout-ncol=2}
```{r fig-figure5a}
#| fig-height: 3.5
#| fig-width: 3.5
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| echo: false

plotrda_regiolandscape
```

```{r fig-figure5b}
#| fig-height: 3.5
#| fig-width: 3.5
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| echo: false

plotrda_fieldfine
```

Ordination plots of RDA models (a) between the regional environment and the landscape matrix and (b) between the field management and the fine-scale environment. Abbreviations: AnnPreci: annual precipitation; Elev: elevation; JulTemp: average July temperature; SeaDist: distance to sea; StockDens: average stocking density; Bryo: percent bryophyte cover; BD: bulk density; SoilPene: soil penetration.
:::

## Relationships between explanatory sets and dominant grass, forb and dung-associated beetle assemblages

Dominant grass assemblages were correlated with the regional environment (rho = `r op(comvar$rho[1], 2)`, F~35,77~ = `r op(comvar$approx[1], 2)`, p-value = `r op(comvar$p.value[1], 3)`), field management (rho = `r op(comvar$rho[3], 2)`, F~28,66~ = `r op(comvar$approx[3], 2)`, p-value = `r op(comvar$p.value[3], 3)`) and the fine-scale environment (rho = `r op(comvar$rho[4], 2)`, F~42,86~ = `r op(comvar$approx[4], 2)`, p-value = `r op(comvar$p.value[4], 3)`) (@fig-cancorgrass; @tbl-cancorcom).
However, only the fine-scale environment explained a significant proportion of the grass assemblage variance (R~adj~^2^ = `r op(rdastat_grass$adj.r.squared[28], 2)`, F~6~ = `r op(rdastat_grass$F[28], 2)`, p-value = `r op(rdastat_grass$PrF[28], 3)`) (@tbl-rdagrass).
The first axis was significant (F = `r op(rdastat_grass$F[30], 2)`, p-value = `r op(rdastat_grass$PrF[30], 3)`) and essentially driven by average vegetation height (F = `r op(rdastat_grass$F[33], 2)`, p-value = `r op(rdastat_grass$PrF[33], 3)`) (@fig-figure6a).
The dominance of *Deschampsia cespitosa* and *Holcus lanatus* in the community was associated with taller vegetation, while the dominance of *Poa pratensis* and *Agrostis capillaris* was rather linked to shorter vegetation.
The second axis was marginally significant (F = `r op(rdastat_grass$F[31], 2)`, p-value = `r op(rdastat_grass$PrF[31], 3)`) and mostly driven a gradient of soil penetration (F = `r op(rdastat_grass$F[37], 2)`, p-value = `r op(rdastat_grass$PrF[37], 3)`) (@tbl-rdagrass).
The dominance of *Deschampsia flexuosa* in the community was associated with lower soil resistance, while the dominance of *Festuca rubra* and *Holcus lanatus* was rather linked to greater soil resistance (@fig-figure6a).
At plot level, the fine-scale environment also explained a significant proportion of the grass assemblage variance (R~adj~^2^ = `r op(rdastat_plot$adj.r.squared[1], 2)`, F~6~ = `r op(rdastat_plot$F[1], 2)`, p-value = `r op(rdastat_plot$PrF[1], 3)`) (@tbl-rdaplot).
Axes were driven by similar environmental gradients to the ones observed at site level (@fig-sup1a), but only soil penetration was found significant (F = `r op(rdastat_plot$F[10], 2)`, p-value = `r op(rdastat_plot$PrF[10], 3)`).

Dominant forb assemblages were only correlated with the fine-scale environment (rho = `r op(comvar$rho[9], 2)` (F~42,86~ = `r op(comvar$approx[9], 2)`, p-value = `r op(comvar$p.value[9], 3)`) (@fig-cancorforb; @tbl-cancorcom), which explained a significant proportion of community variance (R~adj~^2^ = `r op(rdastat_forb$adj.r.squared[28], 2)`, F~6~ = `r op(rdastat_forb$F[28], 2)`, p-value = `r op(rdastat_forb$PrF[28], 3)`) (@tbl-rdaforb).
The first axis was significant (F = `r op(rdastat_forb$F[30], 2)`, p-value = `r op(rdastat_forb$PrF[30], 3)`) and mainly driven by a gradient of soil penetration (F = `r op(rdastat_forb$F[37], 2)`, p-value = `r op(rdastat_forb$PrF[37], 3)`) (@fig-figure6b).
The dominance of *Potentilla erecta* and *Galium saxatile* was associated with lower soil resistance, while the dominance of *Ranunculus acris* and *Trifolium repens* was rather linked to greater soil resistance.
A gradient of vegetation structure was represented on the second axis, which was however non-significant (F = `r op(rdastat_forb$F[31], 2)`, p-value = `r op(rdastat_forb$PrF[31], 3)`) (@tbl-rdaforb).
At plot level, the fine-scale environment also explained a significant proportion of the forb assemblage variance (R~adj~^2^ = `r op(rdastat_plot$adj.r.squared[11], 2)`, F~6~ = `r op(rdastat_plot$F[11], 2)`, p-value = `r op(rdastat_plot$PrF[11], 3)`) (@tbl-rdaplot).
The first axis was driven by a similar gradient of soil penetration to the one observed at site level (F = `r op(rdastat_plot$F[20], 2)`, p-value = `r op(rdastat_plot$PrF[20], 3)`) (@fig-sup1a), which was negatively correlated with bulk density (F = `r op(rdastat_plot$F[17], 2)`, p-value = `r op(rdastat_plot$PrF[17], 3)`) and soil pH (F = `r op(rdastat_plot$F[19], 2)`, p-value = `r op(rdastat_plot$PrF[19], 3)`).

Dominant beetle assemblages were correlated with the regional environment (rho = `r op(comvar$rho[11], 2)`, F~25,87~ = `r op(comvar$approx[11], 2)`, p-value = `r op(comvar$p.value[11], 3)`), the landscape matrix (rho = `r op(comvar$rho[12], 2)`, F~30,82~ = `r op(comvar$approx[12], 2)`, p-value = `r op(comvar$p.value[12], 3)`) and the field management (rho = `r op(comvar$rho[13], 2)`, F~20,74~ = `r op(comvar$approx[13], 2)`, p-value = `r op(comvar$p.value[13], 3)`) (@fig-cancorbeetle; @tbl-cancorcom).
However, only the fine-scale environment explained a significant proportion of beetle assemblage variance (R~adj~^2^ = `r op(rdastat_beetle$adj.r.squared[28], 2)`, F~6~ = `r op(rdastat_beetle$F[28], 2)`, p-value = `r op(rdastat_beetle$PrF[28], 3)`) (@tbl-rdabeetle).
The first axis was marginally significant (F = `r op(rdastat_beetle$F[30], 2)`, p-value = `r op(rdastat_beetle$PrF[30], 3)`) and was essentially driven by a gradient of bulk density (F = `r op(rdastat_beetle$F[34], 2)`, p-value = `r op(rdastat_beetle$PrF[34], 3)`) (@fig-figure6c).
Sites with more compact soils were characterised by a higher relative abundance of Carabidae and a lower relative abundance of Ptiliidae.

:::{#fig-rdacom layout-ncol=2}
``` {r fig-figure6a}
#| fig-height: 3.5
#| fig-width: 3.5
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| echo: false

plotrda_finegrass
```

``` {r fig-figure6b}
#| fig-height: 3.5
#| fig-width: 3.5
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| echo: false

plotrda_fineforb
```

``` {r fig-figure6c}
#| fig-height: 3.5
#| fig-width: 3.5
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| echo: false

plotrda_finebeetle
```

Ordination plots of RDA models (a) between the fine-scale environment and dominant grass assemblages; (b) between the fine-scale environment and dominant forb assemblages; and (c) between the landscape matrix and dominant dung-associated beetle assemblages. Abbreviations: Bryo: percent bryophyte cover; BD: bulk density; SoilPene: soil penetration.
:::

\newpage

# DISCUSSION

We were able to identify both symmetric and directional links between environmental gradients, field management and dominant grass, forb and dung-associated beetle assemblages of semi-natural grasslands in Western Norway.
The composition of the landscape matrix was affected by the geography of the fjord region, with an increase of more intensively managed areas, i.e. infields and croplands, towards the nearest fjord shorelines.
Field management had a marginal influence on the fine-scale environment of semi-natural grasslands, as greater soil compaction and lower vegetation height were weakly associated with sheep grazing and bigger grazing areas.
Dominant grass and beetle assemblages were correlated with the regional environment and field management, but only the fine-scale environment explained significant proportion of community assemblage variance.
Grass and forb assemblages responded to similar gradients of soil penetration and of vegetation structure, while beetle assemblages were rather affected by bulk density.

## Fjord geography influences land use at landscape scale

We expected the regional environment of the fjord region to affect land use due to strong oceanity and elevation gradients affecting plant phenology and soil fertility [@karlsenVariabilityStartGrowing2007; @uttakleivLandskapskartleggingNorgeLandscape2011].
However, we observed that, within the investigated range, the environmental context at the individual fjord level had a stronger influence on landscape composition than broader west-east patterns.
Our findings may reflect how land use history and the evolution of the socioeconomic context have durably imprinted the landscape of the fjord region [@beleHeritageBoundariesNatureculture2024].
Traditional farmer-fisher communities used to rely on both land and water resources to ensure their livelihood, using the fjords not only for fishing but also for the transport of livestock and people [@brekkeKulturhistoriskVegbokHordaland1993; @hetlandPluriactivityStrategyEmployment1986].
Crops and hay-meadows were thus mostly cultivated and managed along the fjords and fjord arms, while less-accessible grasslands and shrublands were used for extensive grazing [@indrelidFarmingSystemIts1988; @olssonAgroecosystemsNeolithicTime1991].
During the post-WW2 agricultural transition, outermost areas were abandoned or converted for the development of the forestry industry, while livestock was moved to more fertile areas [@fjellstadPatternsChangeTwo1999].
Steady land use intensification during the past decades has probably contributed to deepen the changes in the landscape between fjord shorelines and inland areas [@hamreLandcoverStructuralChanges2007].

## Weak relationship between field management and the fine-scale environment

We expected vegetation structure and soil physicochemical parameters to show consistent patterns according to the intensity of the grazing disturbance.
For instance, factors like stocking density or cattle grazing were shown to decrease vegetation height [@lyonsImpactsContrastingConservation2017; @pakemanLongTermImpacts2019] and increase soil compaction [@mayelHowDoesGrassland2021; @zhangSoilPhysicalChemical2019].
However, we observed opposite trends, as greater bulk density and lower vegetation height were instead associated with lower stocking density and sheep grazing.
Shorter vegetation could be associated with a more homogeneous foraging behaviour of sheep, which are more efficient in reaching palatable biomass than cattle [@mohammedGrazingBehaviorDietary2020; @rookMatchingTypeLivestock2004].
Even so, this interpretation could only be verified with an explicit measurement of vegetation heterogeneity neither does it consider other patterns of environmental variables.
Opposite patterns of vegetation height and bulk density could also indicate an underlying gradient of grassland productivity [@orwinLinkagesPlantTraits2010].
Considering that bulk density was negatively correlated with soil carbon and nitrogen contents, more fertile soils would be associated with taller standing vegetation.
Rather than a direct impact of management, our findings may thus reflect how farming models are adapted to the inherent productivity of grazing fields [@demeauxJustGrazeIt2024].
Semi-natural grasslands with slower biomass regeneration capacity tend to be used for extensive grazing of smaller livestock, while more productive fields are mostly selected for more intensive mixed dairy-beef production [@asheimPolicyMeasuresPreserve2020; @potthoffNorwegianLowintensityOvine2023].
However, our analysis showed this relationship to be negligible when maximum variance was considered, indicating the existence of more complex interactions between the local environment, the farmer and the livestock.

## Drivers of community assemblage variances

While both the regional environment and field management were correlated with dominant grass and dung-associated beetle assemblages, none of them directly explained significant proportions of variance.
On the other hand, all community assemblages were influenced by the fine-scale environment of grazing fields.
Grass and beetle assemblages responded to average vegetation height and bulk density respectively, suggesting that both communities were influenced by similar productivity gradients to the one identified hereinabove [@olffEffectsHerbivoresGrassland1998; @orwinLinkagesPlantTraits2010], and subsequently indirectly linked to field management.
Considering the large proportion of unexplained variance in the beetle model though, a finer community analysis at species-level may be necessary to understand the underlying processes operating at fine scale [e.g. @sigsgaardEnvironmentalDNAMetabarcoding2021].
Regardless, these results support that current farming practices in the semi-natural grasslands of the Nordhordland UNESCO Biosphere Reserve do not override the inherent variation in community assemblages driven by the fine-scale environment.

The stronger influence of environmental factors on plant assemblages at fine scale is consistent with previous findings [e.g. @klimekPlantSpeciesRichness2007; @siefertScaleDependenceVegetationenvironment2012], even though our models showed large proportions of unexplained variance [see also @raatikainenImpactsLocalRegional2007].
Interestingly, soil penetration was the only significant predictor of dominant grass and forb community variance across both site- and plot-level, while factors such as available phosphorus only showed negligible contributions [contrary to e.g. @myklestadSoilSiteManagement2004; @kleinebeckerEffectsGrazingSeasonal2011].
Soil penetration was systematically negatively related to a pH gradient while its relationship with bulk density was not consistent across models, suggesting a greater contribution of biological factors (e.g. plant roots) compared with soil physical properties [@forsterRootTraitsCrop2022; @kumiQuantifyingRootinducedSoil2023].
Lower resistance on more acidic soils could be explained by the increased concentration of ammonium and ionic aluminium which inhibit plant root growth [@forsterRootTraitsCrop2022; @kleijnSearchKeyBiogeochemical2008].
However, experimental acidification in grasslands showed instead an increase of root density following a community shift from forbs to perennial grasses [@chenBioticCommunityShifts2015].
We cannot extrapolate further on this soil acidity-resistance relationship without direct assessment of root length/density and standardised measurement of soil resistance to penetration [@sojkaSituStrengthBulk2001], but this interaction would be worth investigating further.

Regarding the relationships between the regional environment and community assemblages, the sensitivity of our analysis to broader gradients was probably limited by the spatial range of our study area (approximately 900 km^2^), for which local climatic and topographic variables could have stronger influence than regional processes [@siefertScaleDependenceVegetationenvironment2012].
Our findings may also indicate the existence of more complex non-linear interactions, which should be investigated with a more robust analytical framework [@dixonhamilCrossscaleContradictionsEcological2016; @petersCrossScaleInteractions2007; @redlichDisentanglingEffectsClimate2022].
In an extensive study on meadow communities in Western Norway, @losvikHayMeadowCommunities1993 observed a divergence between coastal and boreal meadows linked to a fertilisation gradient, suggesting that grasslands further inland were, at the time, more productive and more intensively managed.
We did not include fertilisation (e.g. type, amount) in our analyses due to inconsistent information from the farmers, which could have contributed to explain community variance [@myklestadSoilSiteManagement2004].

Overall, our study is quite representative of how challenging ecological assessments of small-scale grazing systems are in observational studies, particularly when rotational management is involved.
While in theory a *certain number of animals* is allocated on a *certain area of land* for a *certain period of time*, farmers continuously adapt their management to unforeseeable circumstances, such as for instance the livestock birth rate/survival or the timing of spring bloom [@nuthallIntuitiveWorldFarmers2012].
We witnessed it firsthand during our interviews, as the farmers themselves struggled to provide accurate grazing schedules for a single field.
Scaling up the study design from a single representative field to the whole farm matrix, and developing novel indicators based on a mosaic concept, would be more suitable to capture the spatiatemporal dynamics of management [@deffontainesAgriculturalSystemsLandscape1995; @kokEuropeanBiodiversityAssessments2020].

# CONCLUSION

Our study shows how environmental gradients operating at different spatial scales can influence plant and animal communities of semi-natural grasslands in the fjord region of Western Norway.
We found that community assemblages were primarily determined by the fine-scale environment, but responded to different predictors.
There was no evidence of an effect of field management on dominant assemblages, even though our findings suggest the existence of an indirect link mediated by field productivity.
Therefore, our analyses indicate that, in small-scale grazing systems, the effect of diversified grazing management may be overridden by stronger influences of environmental gradients.
Overall, we highlighted the complexity of interactions between the farmer, the environment and the livestock in small-scale grazing systems.
We encourage further research to disentangle these relationships through explicitly addressing the spatiotemporal variability of rotational management.

# ACKNOWLEDGEMENTS

We thank our technicians Linn Voldstad and Silje Östman for administrative support and logistical organisation, and for their help in data collection in the field and in the lab.
We also thank the many interns and volunteers who participated in the field season and helped with lab work: Morgane Demeaux, Micah Elias, Astrid Bjørnsen, Ingrid Vaksvik, Kristi Natås, Natalie Paluchova, Christina Schickhoff, Anette Gilje Oppistov, Maria Galea, Keith McInturff, Johan Måren and Dan Andersson.
We are grateful to the farmers and landowners of the study sites for allowing access to their fields and for their time during the phone interviews.
We also thank Peter Manning for his pertinent insights on the conceptual design, as well as Michael Patten and Richard Telford for their valuable guidance on the data analysis.

# STATEMENTS AND DECLARATIONS

## FUNDING

This work was supported by the Research Council of Norway (grant number 280299); 
and the Czech Academy of Sciences (grant number RVO 67985939).

## AUTHOR CONTRIBUTION

Conceptualization, A.E.E., M.K.;
Methodology, A.E.E., M.K.;
Validation, I.E.M, A.E.E., A.K., M.K.;
Formal analysis, M.K., A.E.E., A.K.;
Data collection, M.K., A.E.E.;
Writing - original draft preparation, M.K.;
Writing - review and editing, I.E.M, A.E.E., A.K.;
Visualization, M.K.;
Supervision, I.E.M., A.E.E.;
Project administration, I.E.M.;
Funding acquisition, I.E.M.

All authors have read and agreed to the final version of the manuscript.

## CONSENT TO PUBLISH

All authors have given consent to publish.

## COMPETING INTERESTS

The authors declare no competing interests.

## DATA AVAILABILITY

The dataset used in this manuscript is planned to be submitted to DataverseNO (https://dataverse.no/).

## CODE AVAILABILITY

The codes of the data curation and analyses are available at

https://github.com/MorganeKerdoncuff/NBRSurvey_TradMod.

\newpage

# REFERENCES

\setlength{\baselineskip}{1pt}
\scriptsize

::: {#refs}
:::

\newpage

# SUPPLEMENTARY INFORMATION

```{=tex}
\appendix
\renewcommand{\thefigure}{S\arabic{figure}}
\renewcommand{\thetable}{S\arabic{table}}
\setcounter{figure}{0}
\setcounter{table}{0}
```

\newpage
\scriptsize

```{r tbl-cancorenv}
#| tbl-cap: "Summary of the asymptotic approximation of Hotelling-Lawley Trace of canonical correlation analyses for investigating links between the regional environment, the landscape matrix, field management and the fine-scale environment of semi-natural grasslands in the Nordhordland UNESCO Biosphere Reserve."
#| message: false
#| warning: false

library(gt)
library(tidyverse)

cancorenv <- subset(envar, select = -c(id, stat))
cancorenv |>
  gt(groupname_col = "explanatory", rowname_col = "response") |>
  cols_move_to_start(
    columns = c("rho", "dim")
  ) |>
  cols_move(
    columns = rho,
    after = dim
  ) |>
  cols_label(
    #stat = "statistic",
    approx = "F-approx",
    p.value = "p-value",
    dim = "dimension"
  ) |>
  fmt_number(
    columns = c("approx", "rho"),
    decimals = 2
  ) |>
  fmt_number(
    columns = p.value,
    decimals = 4
  ) |>
  opt_stylize(style = 3, color = 'gray') |>
    tab_options(
    table.font.size = 11
  )

```

```{r tbl-cancorcom}
#| tbl-cap: "Summary of the asymptotic approximation of Hotelling-Lawley Trace of canonical correlation analyses for investigating links between explanatory sets (the regional environment, the landscape matrix, field management and the fine-scale environment) on dominant grass, forb and dung-associated beetle assemblages of semi-natural grasslands in the Nordhordland UNESCO Biosphere Reserve."
#| message: false
#| warning: false

library(gt)
library(tidyverse)

cancorcom <- subset(comvar, select = -c(id, stat))
cancorcom |>
  gt(groupname_col = "explanatory", rowname_col = "response") |>
  cols_move_to_start(
    columns = c("rho", "dim")
  ) |>
  cols_move(
    columns = rho,
    after = dim
  ) |>
  cols_label(
    #stat = "statistic",
    approx = "F-approx",
    p.value = "p-value",
    dim = "dimension"
  ) |>
  fmt_number(
    columns = c("approx", "rho"),
    decimals = 2
  ) |>
  fmt_number(
    columns = p.value,
    decimals = 4
  ) |>
  opt_stylize(style = 3, color = 'gray') |>
    tab_options(
    table.font.size = 11
  )

```

\newpage
\blandscape

```{r tbl-rdaregio}
#| tbl-cap: "Summary of the RDA permutation tests for investigating links between the regional environment and the landscape matrix, field management and the fine-scale environment of semi-natural grasslands in the Nordhordland UNESCO Biosphere Reserve."
#| message: false
#| warning: false

library(gt)
library(tidyverse)

# Base table
rdafjord <- subset(rdastat_envi, select = -c(r.squared, type, eigenval))
rdafjord <- filter(rdafjord, explanatory == "Regional")

# New columns for table spanner
rdafjord <- rdafjord |>
    pivot_longer(cols = c(adj.r.squared, Variance, F, Df, PrF), names_to = "stat", values_to = "val") |>
  mutate(statset = ifelse(
    response == "Landscape", paste(stat, "lands", sep = ""), ifelse(
      response == "Field", paste(stat, "field", sep = ""), paste(stat, "fine", sep = "")
    )
  ))

# New table
rdafjord <- subset(rdafjord, select = c(dim, statset, val))
rdafjord <- rdafjord |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = statset, values_from = val)

rdafjord |>
  # gt() |>
  gt(rowname_col = "dim") |>
  cols_move_to_start(
    columns = c("adj.r.squaredlands", "adj.r.squaredfield", "adj.r.squaredfine")
  ) |>
  cols_move(
    columns = Dffine,
    after = Ffine
  ) |>
    cols_move(
    columns = Dflands,
    after = Flands
  ) |>
  cols_move(
    columns = Dffield,
    after = Ffield
  ) |>
  cols_label(
    Variancefine = "variance",
    Ffine = "F",
    PrFfine = "p-value",
    Dffine = "df",
    adj.r.squaredfine = "adjRsq",
    Variancelands = "variance",
    Flands = "F",
    PrFlands = "p-value",
    Dflands = "df",
    adj.r.squaredlands = "adjRsq",
    Variancefield = "variance",
    Ffield = "F",
    PrFfield = "p-value",
    Dffield = "df",
    adj.r.squaredfield = "adjRsq"
  ) |>
  fmt_number(
    columns = -c("PrFlands", "PrFfield", "PrFfine", "Dflands", "Dffield", "Dffine"),
    decimals = 2
  ) |>
  fmt_number(
    columns = c("PrFlands", "PrFfield", "PrFfine"),
    decimals = 3
  ) |>
    fmt_number(
    columns = c("Dflands", "Dffield", "Dffine"),
    decimals = 0
  ) |>
      tab_spanner(
      label = "Regional x landscape",
      columns = c("Variancelands", "Flands", "PrFlands", "Dflands", "adj.r.squaredlands")
    ) |>
    tab_spanner(
      label = "Regional x field",
      columns = c("Variancefield", "Ffield", "PrFfield", "Dffield", "adj.r.squaredfield")
    ) |>
    tab_spanner(
      label = "Regional x fine",
      columns = c("Variancefine", "Ffine", "PrFfine", "Dffine", "adj.r.squaredfine")
    ) |>
  sub_missing(missing_text = "-") |>
  opt_stylize(style = 3, color = 'gray') |>
    tab_options(
    table.font.size = 11
  )

```


```{r tbl-rdalandscape}
#| tbl-cap: "Summary of the RDA permutation tests for investigating links between the landscape matrix and field management and the fine-scale environment of semi-natural grasslands in the Nordhordland UNESCO Biosphere Reserve."
#| message: false
#| warning: false

library(gt)
library(tidyverse)

# Base table
rdalandscape <- subset(rdastat_envi, select = -c(r.squared, type, eigenval))
rdalandscape <- filter(rdalandscape, explanatory == "Landscape")

# New columns for table spanner
rdalandscape <- rdalandscape |>
    pivot_longer(cols = c(adj.r.squared, Variance, F, Df, PrF), names_to = "stat", values_to = "val") |>
  mutate(statset = ifelse(
    response == "Field", paste(stat, "field", sep = ""), paste(stat, "fine", sep = "")
  ))

# New table
rdalandscape <- subset(rdalandscape, select = c(dim, statset, val))
rdalandscape <- rdalandscape |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = statset, values_from = val)

rdalandscape |>
  gt(rowname_col = "dim") |>
  cols_move(
    columns = Dffine,
    after = Ffine
  ) |>
  cols_move(
    columns = Dffield,
    after = Ffield
  ) |>
  cols_label(
    Variancefine = "variance",
    Ffine = "F",
    PrFfine = "p-value",
    Dffine = "df",
    adj.r.squaredfine = "adjRsq",
    Variancefield = "variance",
    Ffield = "F",
    PrFfield = "p-value",
    Dffield = "df",
    adj.r.squaredfield = "adjRsq"
  ) |>
  fmt_number(
    columns = -c("PrFfield", "PrFfine", "Dffield", "Dffine"),
    decimals = 2
  ) |>
  fmt_number(
    columns = c("PrFfield", "PrFfine"),
    decimals = 3
  ) |>
    fmt_number(
    columns = c("Dffield", "Dffine"),
    decimals = 0
  ) |>
    tab_spanner(
      label = "Landscape x field",
      columns = c("Variancefield", "Ffield", "PrFfield", "Dffield", "adj.r.squaredfield")
    ) |>
    tab_spanner(
      label = "Landscape x fine",
      columns = c("Variancefine", "Ffine", "PrFfine", "Dffine", "adj.r.squaredfine")
    ) |>
  sub_missing(missing_text = "-") |>
  opt_stylize(style = 3, color = 'gray') |>
  tab_options(
    table.font.size = 11
  )

```

\elandscape

```{r tbl-rdafield}
#| tbl-cap: "Summary of the RDA permutation tests for investigating links between field management and the fine-scale environment of semi-natural grasslands in the Nordhordland UNESCO Biosphere Reserve."
#| message: false
#| warning: false

library(gt)
library(tidyverse)

# Base table
rdafield <- subset(rdastat_envi, select = -c(r.squared, type, eigenval))
rdafield <- filter(rdafield, explanatory == "Field")
rdafield <- subset(rdafield, select = -c(explanatory, model, response))

# Gt table
rdafield |>
  gt(rowname_col = "dim") |>
  cols_move(
    columns = Df,
    after = F
  ) |>
  cols_label(
    Variance = "variance",
    F = "F",
    PrF = "p-value",
    Df = "df",
    adj.r.squared = "adjRsq",
  ) |>
  fmt_number(
    columns = -c("PrF", "Df"),
    decimals = 2
  ) |>
  fmt_number(
    columns = c("PrF"),
    decimals = 3
  ) |>
    fmt_number(
    columns = c("Df"),
    decimals = 0
  ) |>
    tab_spanner(
      label = "Field x fine",
      columns = c("Variance", "F", "PrF", "Df", "adj.r.squared")
    ) |>
  sub_missing(missing_text = "-") |>
  opt_stylize(style = 3, color = 'gray') |>
    tab_options(
    table.font.size = 11
  )

```


```{r tbl-rdagrass}
#| tbl-cap: "Summary of the RDA permutation tests for investigating the influence of the regional environment, the landscape matrix, field management and the fine-scale environment on dominant grass assemblages of semi-natural grasslands in the Nordhordland UNESCO Biosphere Reserve."
#| message: false
#| warning: false

library(gt)
library(tidyverse)

rdagrass <- subset(rdastat_grass, select = -c(r.squared, type, eigenval, explanatory, response))
rdagrass |>
  gt(groupname_col = "model", rowname_col = "dim") |>
  cols_move_to_start(
    columns = c("adj.r.squared")
  ) |>
  cols_move(
    columns = Df,
    after = F
  ) |>
  cols_label(
    Variance = "variance",
    PrF = "p-value",
    Df = "df",
    adj.r.squared = "adjRsq"
  ) |>
  fmt_number(
    columns = c("Variance", "F", "adj.r.squared"),
    decimals = 2
  ) |>
  fmt_number(
    columns = PrF,
    decimals = 4
  ) |>
  sub_missing(missing_text = "-") |>
  opt_stylize(style = 3, color = 'gray') |>
  tab_options(
    table.font.size = 11
  )

```


```{r tbl-rdaforb}
#| tbl-cap: "Summary of the RDA permutation tests for investigating the influence of the regional environment, the landscape matrix, field management and the fine-scale environment on dominant forb assemblages of semi-natural grasslands in the Nordhordland UNESCO Biosphere Reserve."
#| message: false
#| warning: false

library(gt)
library(tidyverse)

rdaforb <- subset(rdastat_forb, select = -c(r.squared, type, eigenval, explanatory, response))
rdaforb |>
  gt(groupname_col = "model", rowname_col = "dim") |>
  cols_move_to_start(
    columns = c("adj.r.squared")
  ) |>
  cols_move(
    columns = Df,
    after = F
  ) |>
  cols_label(
    Variance = "variance",
    PrF = "p-value",
    Df = "df",
    adj.r.squared = "adjRsq"
  ) |>
  fmt_number(
    columns = c("Variance", "F", "adj.r.squared"),
    decimals = 2
  ) |>
  fmt_number(
    columns = PrF,
    decimals = 4
  ) |>
  sub_missing(missing_text = "-") |>
  opt_stylize(style = 3, color = 'gray') |>
    tab_options(
    table.font.size = 11
  )

```


```{r tbl-rdabeetle}
#| tbl-cap: "Summary of the RDA permutation tests for investigating the influence of the regional environment, the landscape matrix, field management and the fine-scale environment on dominant dung-associated beetle assemblages of semi-natural grasslands in the Nordhordland UNESCO Biosphere Reserve."
#| message: false
#| warning: false

library(gt)
library(tidyverse)

rdabeetle <- subset(rdastat_beetle, select = -c(r.squared, type, eigenval, explanatory, response))
rdabeetle |>
  gt(groupname_col = "model", rowname_col = "dim") |>
  cols_move_to_start(
    columns = c("adj.r.squared")
  ) |>
  cols_move(
    columns = Df,
    after = F
  ) |>
  cols_label(
    Variance = "variance",
    PrF = "p-value",
    Df = "df",
    adj.r.squared = "adjRsq"
  ) |>
  fmt_number(
    columns = c("Variance", "F", "adj.r.squared"),
    decimals = 2
  ) |>
  fmt_number(
    columns = PrF,
    decimals = 4
  ) |>
  sub_missing(missing_text = "-") |>
  opt_stylize(style = 3, color = 'gray') |>
    tab_options(
    table.font.size = 11
  )

```


```{r tbl-rdaplot}
#| tbl-cap: "Summary of supplementary RDA permutation tests for investigating the influence of the fine-scale environment on dominant grass and forb assemblages of semi-natural grasslands in the Nordhordland UNESCO Biosphere Reserve at plot-level."
#| message: false
#| warning: false

library(gt)
library(tidyverse)

rdagrass <- subset(rdastat_plot, select = -c(r.squared, type, eigenval, explanatory, response))
rdagrass |>
  gt(groupname_col = "model", rowname_col = "dim") |>
  cols_move_to_start(
    columns = c("adj.r.squared")
  ) |>
  cols_move(
    columns = Df,
    after = F
  ) |>
  cols_label(
    Variance = "variance",
    PrF = "p-value",
    Df = "df",
    adj.r.squared = "adjRsq"
  ) |>
  fmt_number(
    columns = c("Variance", "F", "adj.r.squared"),
    decimals = 2
  ) |>
  fmt_number(
    columns = PrF,
    decimals = 4
  ) |>
  sub_missing(missing_text = "-") |>
  opt_stylize(style = 3, color = 'gray') |>
  tab_options(
    table.font.size = 11
  )

```

\newpage

:::{#fig-rdaplot layout-ncol=2}
``` {r fig-sup1a}
#| fig-height: 3.5
#| fig-width: 3.5
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| echo: false

plotrda_finegrassplot
```

``` {r fig-sup1b}
#| fig-height: 3.5
#| fig-width: 3.5
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| echo: false

plotrda_fineforbplot
```

Ordination plots of supplementary RDA models (a) between the fine-scale environment and dominant grass assemblages and (b) between the fine-scale environment and dominant forb assemblages at plot level. Abbreviations: Bryo: percent bryophyte cover; BD: bulk density; SoilPene: soil penetration.
:::
